one_liner: A 19-year-old college freshman presents with 10 days of severe fatigue, sore throat with tonsillar exudates, posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, and splenomegaly.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient initially thought he had a cold when he developed a sore throat and low-grade fever about 10 days ago. Symptoms have progressively worsened despite ibuprofen and rest. He now has severe fatigue, difficulty swallowing due to throat pain (8/10), intermittent fevers up to 38.8°C, and loss of appetite with 2 kg weight loss. He reports a mild headache and vague left upper quadrant discomfort. He was prescribed amoxicillin by an urgent care clinic 5 days ago for presumed strep throat, after which he developed a diffuse maculopapular rash. He is sexually active with one female partner. No recent travel. He shares drinks frequently at social events.
stage2.pmh: No significant past medical history. Immunizations up to date.
stage2.medications: Amoxicillin 500 mg TID (started 5 days ago, now discontinued). Ibuprofen 400 mg PRN.
stage2.social_history: College freshman living in dormitory. Social alcohol on weekends. Non-smoker. No illicit drugs. Sexually active with one female partner, uses condoms inconsistently.
stage2.physical_exam: Fatigued-appearing young male. Bilateral tonsillar enlargement (3+) with grayish-white exudates. Palatal petechiae noted. Prominent bilateral posterior cervical lymphadenopathy (1.5-2 cm, tender, mobile). Anterior cervical nodes also mildly enlarged. Fading maculopapular rash over trunk and arms (attributed to amoxicillin). Mild periorbital edema bilaterally. Abdomen soft, spleen tip palpable 3 cm below the left costal margin, mildly tender. No hepatomegaly. No jaundice. No axillary or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Lymphocytosis with >10% atypical lymphocytes is highly characteristic of EBV this condition
Positive heterophile antibody confirms infectious this condition
Positive VCA IgM indicates acute EBV infection
VCA IgG appears early and persists for life; positive in acute and past infection
Negative EBNA antibody supports acute (not past) EBV infection — EBNA appears weeks to months after onset
Mild to moderate transaminitis occurs in ~80% of EBV this condition cases
Mildly elevated bilirubin; can occur with EBV hepatitis
Excludes Group A Streptococcus as cause of pharyngitis
Mild thrombocytopenia seen in 25-50% of mono cases

imaging_results.result: Spleen enlarged at 15.2 cm (normal <13 cm). Homogeneous echotexture, no focal lesions. Liver normal in size and appearance.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Confirms splenomegaly; important for counseling on activity restrictions to prevent splenic rupture

key_tests: CBC with differential (looking for atypical lymphocytosis)
EBV-specific serologies (VCA IgM, VCA IgG, EBNA IgG) if monospot negative
acceptable_tests: Rapid strep test or throat culture (to exclude GAS co-infection)
low_yield_tests: CT neck (not indicated for reactive lymphadenopathy)
Lymph node biopsy (unnecessary with classic presentation and positive serology)
Antistreptolysin O (ASO) titer (does not change acute management)

stage5.final_clue: Classic EBV infectious this condition: a young adult with the triad of pharyngitis with tonsillar exudates, posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, and splenomegaly. Supported by >10% atypical lymphocytes, positive monospot, acute EBV serologic profile (VCA IgM positive, EBNA negative), transaminitis, thrombocytopenia, and a maculopapular rash triggered by amoxicillin (classic aminopenicillin rash of mono).

debrief.classic_presentation: A young adult (15-25 years) presenting with the classic triad: exudative pharyngitis, posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, and fatigue/fever lasting 1-4 weeks. Splenomegaly is present in ~50% of cases. Palatal petechiae and periorbital edema are supportive findings. A maculopapular rash occurring after aminopenicillin administration (amoxicillin/ampicillin) is a classic association. Labs show lymphocytosis with >10% atypical lymphocytes and mild transaminitis.
debrief.pathophysiology: Epstein-Barr virus (HHV-4) is transmitted through saliva ('kissing disease'). EBV infects oropharyngeal epithelial cells and then B lymphocytes via the CD21 receptor. Infected B cells undergo polyclonal activation and proliferation, disseminating virus to lymphoid tissue throughout the body. The characteristic atypical lymphocytes are actually activated CD8+ cytotoxic T cells responding to EBV-infected B cells (not the infected B cells themselves). This robust T-cell response causes lymph node enlargement and splenomegaly. Heterophile antibodies (detected by monospot) are IgM antibodies produced by activated B cells that cross-react with antigens on horse, sheep, and bovine red blood cells. The transaminitis reflects hepatic involvement from immune-mediated inflammation. The aminopenicillin rash is thought to result from transient immune dysregulation, not a true drug allergy.
debrief.treatment_overview: Supportive care is the mainstay: adequate rest, hydration, acetaminophen or NSAIDs for fever and pain. Avoid contact sports and strenuous activity for at least 3-4 weeks (or until splenomegaly resolves) due to risk of splenic rupture. Corticosteroids (prednisone 40-60 mg/day tapered over 1-2 weeks) are reserved for complications: impending airway obstruction from tonsillar hypertrophy, severe thrombocytopenia, hemolytic anemia, or myocarditis. Discontinue amoxicillin (aminopenicillin rash is NOT a contraindication to future penicillin use — document as 'aminopenicillin rash with EBV,' not a penicillin allergy). Counsel patient on prolonged fatigue (weeks to months) and viral shedding in saliva (intermittent, lifelong).
high_yield_pearls: The aminopenicillin rash in EBV mononucleosis is NOT a true penicillin allergy and should NOT be listed as such in the allergy history.
The monospot (heterophile antibody) can be falsely negative in the first week of illness and in children under 4 years; use EBV-specific serologies if clinical suspicion is high.
Atypical lymphocytes on CBC are activated CD8+ T cells, not infected B cells.
Splenic rupture is the most feared complication — restrict contact sports and heavy lifting for 3-4 weeks; most ruptures occur in weeks 2-3 of illness.
Always consider acute HIV in a mono-like illness — if monospot is negative or the patient has risk factors, order HIV testing.
sources: Luzuriaga K, Sullivan JL. Infectious mononucleosis. N Engl J Med. 2010;362(21):1993-2000.
Ebell MH et al. Does this patient have infectious mononucleosis? The rational clinical examination. JAMA. 2016;315(14):1502-1509.

after_stage2_more_likely: EBV mononucleosis — classic triad of exudative pharyngitis, posterior cervical lymphadenopathy, and splenomegaly in a college student; aminopenicillin rash strongly suggestive
CMV mononucleosis — similar syndrome but typically has less prominent pharyngitis
after_stage2_less_likely: Group A streptococcal pharyngitis — would not explain splenomegaly, diffuse lymphadenopathy, or aminopenicillin rash
Lymphoma — acute onset with fever and infectious symptoms makes reactive process far more likely
after_labs_top3: EBV mononucleosis — positive monospot, VCA IgM positive with EBNA negative (acute profile), >10% atypical lymphocytes, transaminitis, splenomegaly on ultrasound
CMV mononucleosis — excluded by positive EBV-specific serologies
Acute HIV — should be considered but EBV confirmed; HIV screening still reasonable to perform
